one_line_takeaway: A systematic empirical replication-and-extension study classifies and measures how character/word/sentence-level perturbations to code, task descriptions, and comments degrade functional-correctness robustness (Pass@k / RPs@k / RDs@k / RRs@k) across six LLM4Code models on HumanEval and MBPP, finding word-level perturbations are the most damaging and that general-purpose Llama-3 and monolingual models are more robust than code-specialized or multilingual counterparts -- but it never actually tests whether perturbations induce insecure code, despite framing itself with a security threat model.
key_finding: Sentence/statement-level perturbations were generally handled with the highest resilience across models; word-level perturbations caused the most consistent degradation; character-level perturbations produced mixed results. Concretely on HumanEval (code perturbations, Table 5): Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct had median RP@1 of 0.616 (character), 0.616 (word), and 0.652 (statement) -- the best performer overall -- while CodeLlama-7b-hf had median RP@1 of 0.113 (character), 0.110 (word), and 0.152 (statement) -- the worst performer. On MBPP (Table 7), Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct's median RP values were 0.589 (character), 0.606 (word), 0.604 (statement), with RD of 0.17% (word) vs 0.51% (statement). A comparison against ReCode's originally reported numbers for codegen-2B-mono under BackTranslation found divergent results (this paper: RP@1=0.25, RD@1=0.00%, RR@1=1.22%; ReCode's reported: 0.213, 7.89%, 4.27%), which the authors attribute to sensitivity to dataset version, tokenizer, and decoding-strategy differences between replications. General-purpose Llama-3 outperformed code-specialized CodeLlama in several robustness scenarios, and monolingual model variants (e.g., codegen-2B-mono) outperformed their multilingual counterparts (e.g., codegen-2B-multi) in robustness.
core_contribution: The paper (Liu, Foundjem, Khomh, Li, Polytechnique Montreal) introduces a two-dimensional classification system for adversarial perturbations against Large Language Models for Code (LLM4Code): one axis is content type (code, natural-language task descriptions, comments) and the other is granularity (character-, word-, sentence/statement-level). Building on and replicating ReCode (Wang et al.) and reusing OpenAttack, the authors add six new NLP-derived perturbation techniques (including multi-level character+word perturbations), extend evaluation to Llama-3-8B-Instruct and CodeLlama-7b-hf alongside CodeGen, InCoder, and GPT-J families (6 models total across 5 families), and run all of it on HumanEval (164 problems) and MBPP (974 problems). They report that Llama-3 (a general-purpose model) outperforms CodeLlama (a code-specialized model) in several robustness scenarios, and that monolingual (mono) models outperform multilingual (multi) variants of the same family. The paper also proposes a threat model (Section 2.2, built on Jha et al.'s CodeAttack threat model) for adversarial attacks on deployed LLM4Code, and derives implications for six stakeholder groups (developers, QA engineers, security analysts/red teamers, LLM4Code developers, researchers, engineering managers).
threat_model: Adversary manipulates input prompts to a deployed LLM4Code system (company-owned or public server) via two vectors: (1) server-side compromise injecting modified prompts into queries, or (2) an insider with legitimate access altering inputs before submission. The adversary operates in a black-box setting (no access to model parameters, architecture, or training data; only input-output interaction, mirroring API-only access to systems like Copilot or CodeLlama). Modifications range from character-level (typos, encoding manipulation) to semantic changes (misleading comments, function renaming, logic distortion). Adversary goals: functional errors, security vulnerabilities in generated code (e.g., SQL injection, buffer overflow -- named as examples but not actually produced/tested in this paper), bias introduction, and denial-of-service via malformed inputs. Proposed mitigations are largely generic best practices (input validation, adversarial fine-tuning, dynamic code analysis of outputs, comment/documentation validation) rather than mechanisms the paper builds or evaluates.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 8.1): (1) the perturbation space is bounded by what ReCode/OpenAttack support (character/word/sentence-level on code, docstrings, comments) and does not cover deeper semantic threats such as logic refactoring, inter-procedural changes, or multi-module interactions; (2) only publicly accessible models under 8B parameters were evaluated, excluding frontier commercial models (GPT-4, Claude are named); (3) robustness is measured only via test-case pass/fail functional correctness, not security properties, code quality, or maintainability of the generated code -- so despite the security framing, no generated outputs were actually checked for vulnerabilities like SQL injection or buffer overflow; (4) all experiments are Python-only, limiting cross-language generalizability; (5) models are treated fully black-box, so observed vulnerabilities cannot be attributed to specific architectural or training-data causes; (6) the paper itself notes no standardized robustness benchmark/threat model for LLM4Code yet exists. Additionally (not stated by authors but evident from the text): the threat model's stated adversary goals include "security vulnerabilities" and "bias introduction," but the paper's actual experiments measure only functional-correctness pass rates, so these two goal categories are asserted rather than empirically tested; the manual semantic-validation step, while statistically sized (381/38,692 samples), was performed by the paper's own authors rather than independent annotators, which the paper does not flag as a threat to validity.

datasets_used: HumanEval (164 Python programming problems with function signature, docstring, test cases, canonical solution)
MBPP - Mostly Basic Python Problems (974 Python programming tasks with natural-language prompt and reference solution)
quantitative_results: 38,692 total perturbed instances generated (HumanEval: 4,592 via 28 ReCode methods + 984 via 6 OpenAttack methods; MBPP: 27,272 via ReCode + 5,844 via OpenAttack)
Manual semantic-validation sample: 381 instances (298 ReCode, 82 OpenAttack) at 95% confidence / 5% margin of error
HumanEval, code perturbations (Table 5): Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct median RPs@1 = 0.616 (character), 0.616 (word), 0.652 (statement) -- best performer
HumanEval, code perturbations (Table 5): CodeLlama-7b-hf median RPs@1 = 0.113 (character), 0.110 (word), 0.152 (statement) -- worst performer
HumanEval word- vs character-level RDs@k comparison for Llama-3 tie-break: RDs@k = 1.83% (word) vs 0.92% (character)
MBPP (Table 7): Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct median RPs@k = 0.589 (character), 0.606 (word), 0.604 (statement); RDs@k = 0.17% (word) vs 0.51% (statement)
codegen-2B-mono under BackTranslation, this study vs. ReCode's original report: RPs@1 0.25 vs 0.213; RDs@1 0.00% vs 7.89%; RRs@1 1.22% vs 4.27%
Statistical testing (RQ1/RQ2, Wilcoxon signed-rank + Bonferroni correction): majority of p-values < 0.05, >87% of comparisons show large effect sizes
6 evaluated models across 5 families: CodeGen-2B-Mono, CodeGen-2B-Multi, InCoder, GPT-J, CodeLlama-7b-hf, Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct (CodeT5 excluded due to all-zero evaluation metrics)
34 total perturbation methods used: 28 from ReCode + 6 from OpenAttack, spanning character/word/sentence(statement) granularity across code, task-description, and comment content types
baselines_compared: ReCode (Wang et al.) -- replicated as the code-perturbation and metric-definition baseline (RPs@k, RDs@k, RRs@k)
OpenAttack -- reused as the NLP-perturbation toolkit for task-description attacks, extended with 6 additional high-success-rate methods (PWWS, SememePSO, FD, TextBugger, HotFlip, DeepWordBug)
CodeAttack (Jha et al.) -- source of the adopted threat model, not re-implemented as an attack baseline

Prior LLM4Code robustness comparisons: Mastropaolo et al. (GitHub Copilot resilience to faulty/incomplete prompts) and Improta et al. (data augmentation for offensive code generators) discussed as related but not directly re-run
